title: The Pad Token
concept: We append a special '<PAD>' token to shorter sentences, which tells the model that these positions contain no real data.

exercises.prompt: Computers work best when data is uniform. To help the computer, we want all our sentences to be: sentences in a group should ideally, we want all our input sentences to be: sentences of:
exercises.explain: To process text efficiently, computers need data to be organized in neat, uniform shapes. The pad token helps us achieve this by making all text sequences the same length.

cards.title: The Sentence Puzzle
cards.body: Computers love patterns and neat grids. But human sentences come in all shapes and sizes—some are short, some are long! How do we feed these uneven sentences to an AI without confusing it? We use a clever trick called padding!

cards.title: What is a Pad Token?
cards.body: A pad token (often written as `<PAD>`) is a special placeholder added to the end of shorter sentences. It tells the computer: 'Ignore this part, it is just here to fill space!' This ensures all sentences in a batch are exactly the same length.

cards.title: The Bubble Wrap of AI
cards.body: Imagine packing fragile plates into boxes. If a box isn't full, you stuff it with bubble wrap. The bubble wrap takes up space so things don't shake around, but it isn't a plate. The pad token is just like bubble wrap, filling empty spots to keep your sentence rows neat!

cards.title: Padding in Action
cards.body:
Imagine our AI processes sentences in groups of 5 words. 

If we have:
1. "I love coding today" (4 words)
2. "Yes" (1 word)

With padding, they become:
1. "I love coding today <PAD>"
2. "Yes <PAD> <PAD> <PAD> <PAD>"

Now both are exactly 5 words long!

cards.title: Does Padding Confuse the AI?
cards.body: Some people think adding pad tokens will confuse the AI or change the meaning of a sentence. In reality, modern AI models are trained to completely ignore `<PAD>` tokens when understanding the text. They are just empty space!

cards.title: Visualizing Padding
cards.body: See how sentences of different lengths are made equal using pad tokens.

exercises.prompt: Why do we add pad tokens to shorter sentences?

options.text: To make all sentences the same length for easy processing.
To make the sentences sound more polite.
To correct grammar mistakes in the text.
To translate the sentences into code.

exercises.explain: AI models process data in neat, fixed-size grids. Padding makes sure shorter sentences match the length of the longest sentence so they fit into the grid.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A pad token adds new meaning to a sentence.
exercises.explain: The pad token carries no actual meaning. It is only used as a placeholder to make sentences the same length.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation.
exercises.sentence: To make all sentences the same length, we append a _ token to the shorter ones.
exercises.explain: We use a pad token to fill in the empty spaces of shorter sentences so that they match the length of the longest sentence.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of a pad token is correct?

options.text: The pad token is a special word that teaches the AI the most important meaning of the sentence.
The pad token is just a blank filler that helps make sentences the same length so the computer can process them together.
The pad token is used to mark the exact middle of a sentence.

exercises.explain: The first option is generated by an AI that doesn't understand padding; pad tokens are meaningless placeholders, not the most important words! The second option correctly explains that padding is just for matching sizes.

exercises.prompt: You are building an AI chatbot. Why would you want to add a `<PAD>` token to the end of a shorter sentence?

options.text: It highlights the most important word in each sentence.
It acts as a placeholder so all sentences reach the same length.
It translates sentences into a foreign language.

exercises.explain: The pad token acts as a placeholder so that every sentence is processed at the same length. It does not carry any actual meaning or contribute to the sentence's content.
